title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita

Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

This line chart traces the historical real gross domestic product per capita for six countries from the year 1000 to 2018, with values measured in 2011 US dollars to allow for Purchasing Power Parity comparisons across time. The vertical axis ranges from $0 to $40,000. For the majority of the timeline, spanning from 1000 until approximately 1800, the lines for Italy, the United Kingdom, Japan, China, and India remain clustered in the lower section of the chart, generally fluctuating below the $5,000 mark. Italy’s line, shown in green, exhibits some variation between 1300 and 1700 but stays within this lower band. Beginning in the 19th century, the trajectories for Japan, the United Kingdom, and Italy diverge sharply from the baseline, curving upward in a steep ascent. By 2018, the lines for Japan and the United Kingdom reach the highest points on the chart, terminating between $35,000 and $40,000. Italy’s line also rises significantly but ends slightly lower, near the $35,000 level. In contrast, the lines for China and India remain near the bottom of the chart for a longer duration before showing a sharp upward turn in the most recent decades. China’s line rises to approximately $15,000 by 2018, while India’s line reaches a lower point, sitting below $10,000. The visual pattern highlights a distinct separation in the later years between the three countries that reached the upper bands and the two that remained in the mid-to-lower ranges.
